cards.definition: a family unit consisting of two parents and their children living together
a family unit that includes relatives beyond parents and children — e.g. grandparents, aunts, uncles
a 'blended' family formed when partners with children from previous relationships form a new family unit
a family with one parent raising children — the most common type is a single mother
a family headed by two parents of the same gender, increasingly recognised legally
a sociological perspective that sees the family as essential — performing key functions for society
a conflict perspective seeing the family as serving the interests of capitalism — reproducing inequality
a perspective arguing the family is a site of patriarchy — women perform unpredictable, unequal domestic labour
the decline of religious influence in society — has contributed to rising divorce rates and family diversity
the roles played by husbands and wives within a marriage — joint (shared) or segregated (separate)

questions.question: What is a nuclear family?
options: A) A family with many generations living together
B) Two parents and their children
C) A single parent and children
D) Any family unit
questions.explanation: The nuclear family consists of two parents and their dependent children — traditionally seen as the 'typical' family.

questions.question: What is a reconstituted family?
options: A) A family that has moved house
B) A blended family from partners with children from previous relationships
C) An extended family that has split up
D) A same-sex family
questions.explanation: Reconstituted (blended) families form when partners who already have children from previous relationships form a new family unit.

questions.question: What does functionalism say about the family?
options: A) The family reproduces inequality
B) The family oppresses women
C) The family performs essential functions for society
D) The family is irrelevant to society
questions.explanation: Functionalists like Parsons argue the family performs key functions: primary socialisation and stabilisation of adult personalities.

questions.question: What does feminism say about the family?
options: A) The family is beneficial for women
B) The family is a site of patriarchy — women do unpaid, unequal domestic labour
C) The family serves capitalism
D) The family is the ideal social unit
questions.explanation: Feminists argue the family benefits men — women do the 'triple shift' (paid work, domestic labour, emotional work) while men benefit from their labour.

questions.question: What is secularisation?
options: A) An increase in religious attendance
B) The decline of religious influence in society
C) The growth of non-religious ceremonies
D) The spread of Christianity
questions.explanation: Secularisation has led to more liberal attitudes — contributing to rising divorce rates, cohabitation, and greater family diversity as religious norms weaken.

questions.question: What are conjugal roles?
options: A) Roles played by children in families
B) Roles of husbands and wives within marriage
C) Roles of extended family members
D) Roles of parents in schools
questions.explanation: Conjugal roles are either joint (shared tasks) or segregated (separate tasks for husband and wife). Wilmott and Young argued roles have become increasingly joint.

questions.question: What is a lone parent family?
options: A) A family where one parent works abroad
B) A family with one parent raising children
C) A nuclear family where one parent is deceased
D) A family with one child
questions.explanation: Lone parent families are most commonly headed by single mothers. Their numbers have increased due to rising divorce rates and changing social attitudes.

questions.question: What does Marxism say about the family?
options: A) The family is a positive institution
B) The family serves capitalism by reproducing compliant workers and passing on wealth
C) The family performs primary socialisation
D) The family has no economic function
questions.explanation: Marxists argue the family maintains capitalism — it socialises children to accept inequality and transmits wealth/status between generations.

questions.question: What is an extended family?
options: A) A nuclear family that has grown
B) A family including relatives beyond parents and children
C) A family with more than two children
D) A family that extends across different countries
questions.explanation: Extended families include grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins. They may be vertically extended (multiple generations) or horizontally extended (same generation).

questions.question: What is the 'triple shift' concept?
options: A) Three generations living together
B) Women doing paid work, domestic labour, and emotional work
C) Three types of family structure
D) Shift work affecting family life
questions.explanation: The triple shift (Hochschild) describes women's threefold burden: paid employment, domestic labour (cooking, cleaning), and emotional labour (managing family relationships).

questions.question: A nuclear family consists of two parents and their children.
questions.explanation: The nuclear family is the traditional family unit — though it is no longer the most common form.

questions.question: Functionalists see the family as a site of oppression.
questions.explanation: Functionalists see the family as performing essential positive functions. Feminists see it as oppressive.

questions.question: The reconstituted family has increased due to rising divorce rates.
questions.explanation: More divorces mean more remarriages — creating stepfamilies or reconstituted families.

questions.question: Marxists argue the family primarily benefits women.
questions.explanation: Marxists argue the family benefits CAPITALISM — reproducing a compliant workforce and transmitting property.

questions.question: Feminists argue women perform an unequal share of domestic labour.
questions.explanation: Feminist sociologists argue women do more domestic and emotional work — even when in paid employment (the triple shift).

questions.question: Secularisation has led to more rigid family structures.
questions.explanation: Secularisation has contributed to GREATER diversity — as religious norms against divorce, cohabitation, and same-sex relationships have weakened.

questions.question: Conjugal roles are the roles played by partners within a marriage.
questions.explanation: Joint conjugal roles (shared tasks) have increased as society has changed — though feminists argue true equality has not been achieved.

questions.question: Extended families include only grandparents.
questions.explanation: Extended families include all relatives beyond parents and children — grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins.

questions.question: Same-sex families have become more socially accepted over recent decades.
questions.explanation: Legal recognition (civil partnerships, same-sex marriage) and changing social attitudes have increased acceptance of same-sex families.

questions.question: Lone parent families are most commonly headed by fathers.
questions.explanation: The vast majority of lone parent families are headed by mothers — reflecting wider gender inequalities.

questions.question: A ________ family consists of two parents and their dependent children.
questions.explanation: The nuclear family was long seen as the 'norm' but family diversity has increased significantly.

questions.question: A ________ family includes relatives beyond parents and children — e.g. grandparents.
questions.explanation: Extended families may be vertically (multiple generations) or horizontally (same generation) extended.

questions.question: A ________ family is formed when partners with children from previous relationships combine.
questions.explanation: Also called blended families — their number has grown as divorce and remarriage have increased.

questions.question: ________ see the family as performing essential functions for individuals and society.
questions.explanation: Parsons identified primary socialisation and stabilisation of adult personalities as key family functions.

questions.question: ________ argue the family is a site of patriarchy — women perform unequal domestic labour.
questions.explanation: The 'triple shift' describes women's threefold burden: paid work, domestic labour, and emotional work.

questions.question: The decline of religious influence in society is called ________.
questions.explanation: Secularisation has contributed to greater family diversity — weakening norms against divorce and cohabitation.

questions.question: ________ roles are the roles played by partners within a marriage — joint or segregated.
questions.explanation: Joint roles involve sharing tasks; segregated roles involve separate, traditional divisions.

questions.question: ________ argue the family reproduces capitalism by transmitting wealth and socialising workers.
questions.explanation: The family passes on class position and teaches acceptance of inequality.

questions.question: A ________ parent family has one parent raising children — most commonly a single mother.
questions.explanation: Lone parent families have grown due to rising divorce and changing attitudes to unmarried parenthood.

questions.question: The ________ shift describes women doing paid work, domestic labour, and emotional work.
questions.explanation: Hochschild's concept shows women face a threefold burden even when in full-time employment.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: two parents and their children
family performs essential functions for society
family is a site of patriarchy — women do unequal labour
family serves capitalism — reproduces inequality
blended family from partners with previous children

cards.definition: the idea that success is based on ability and effort — education is supposed to create a level playing field
Bourdieu's concept — middle-class children have knowledge, skills, and attitudes that give them an educational advantage
lack of money and resources — affects working-class pupils' achievement through poor diet, housing, and lack of materials
when teachers or others attach a fixed definition to a pupil — can become a self-fulfilling prophecy
when a label causes a person to behave in ways that confirm the label — e.g. a labelled 'troublemaker' acts out
the informal lessons schools teach — conformity, punctuality, hierarchy — serving middle-class values
grouping pupils by ability across all subjects — research suggests it widens the gap between high and low achievers
some pupils form anti-school subcultures rejecting school values — particularly working-class boys (Willis 1977)
girls consistently outperform boys in exams — explained by changing expectations, feminism, and laddish culture
the policies and practices of institutions that systematically disadvantage ethnic minority groups

questions.question: What is meritocracy?
options: A) A system where the wealthy succeed
B) A system where success is based on ability and effort
C) A system controlled by the middle class
D) A system where birth determines success
questions.explanation: Meritocracy claims education gives everyone an equal chance to succeed based on merit. Sociologists question this — arguing structural inequalities persist.

questions.question: What is cultural capital?
options: A) Money invested in education
B) Middle-class knowledge, skills, and attitudes that give educational advantages
C) The official curriculum taught in schools
D) Resources available to working-class pupils
questions.explanation: Bourdieu argued middle-class children have cultural capital — knowledge of art, language, and cultural norms valued by schools — giving them a built-in advantage.

questions.question: What is a self-fulfilling prophecy?
options: A) Achieving something through hard work
B) A label causing behaviour that confirms the label
C) A teacher's expectation being disproved
D) A pupil choosing to underachieve
questions.explanation: If a teacher labels a pupil as bright, they receive more attention and achieve more. If labelled negatively, they may internalise the label and behave accordingly.

questions.question: What is the hidden curriculum?
options: A) Subjects not taught in schools
B) Informal lessons in conformity, punctuality, and hierarchy serving middle-class values
C) The official exam syllabus
D) Extra-curricular activities
questions.explanation: The hidden curriculum teaches pupils to accept authority, follow rules, and conform — reinforcing middle-class norms and values.

questions.question: What is institutional racism?
options: A) Individual teachers being racist
B) Pupils being deliberately excluded
C) Policies and practices that systematically disadvantage ethnic minority groups
D) Racism that is hidden within schools
questions.explanation: Institutional racism (Macpherson) refers to systems and structures that disadvantage minority groups — even without individual intent.

questions.question: What does Willis's research (1977) show?
options: A) Working-class boys succeed in education
B) Middle-class girls outperform all other groups
C) Working-class boys form anti-school subcultures that reinforce their own class position
D) Streaming benefits all pupils equally
questions.explanation: Willis studied working-class boys who rejected school values, forming a counter-culture ('the lads'). Ironically, this led them into the same working-class jobs as their fathers.

questions.question: What is material deprivation?
options: A) Lack of cultural knowledge
B) Lack of money and resources affecting educational achievement
C) Emotional neglect at home
D) Schools lacking equipment
questions.explanation: Material deprivation affects achievement through: poor diet (affecting concentration), overcrowded housing (no study space), inability to afford resources (books, trips, uniforms).

questions.question: What is labelling in education?
options: A) Naming subjects in school
B) Teachers attaching a fixed definition to a pupil — affecting their achievement
C) Grouping pupils by ability
D) Giving pupils grades
questions.explanation: Labelling theory (Becker) shows that teacher expectations affect pupil behaviour — labelled 'bright' students get more support; labelled 'troublemakers' get less.

questions.question: Why do girls outperform boys in education?
options: A) Girls are naturally more intelligent
B) Boys receive more resources
C) Changing expectations, feminist influence, and boys' 'laddish' anti-school culture
D) Girls attend better schools
questions.explanation: Girls' achievement has risen due to feminism, changing aspirations, and the service economy. Boys are held back by 'laddish' culture that sees academic effort as unmasculine.

questions.question: What is streaming?
options: A) Watching educational videos
B) Grouping pupils by ability across all subjects
C) Teaching pupils in mixed-ability groups
D) Selecting pupils for grammar schools
questions.explanation: Streaming groups pupils by perceived ability for all subjects. Research shows it can become a self-fulfilling prophecy — pupils in lower streams have lower expectations.

questions.question: Meritocracy means success is guaranteed by social class.
questions.explanation: Meritocracy argues success should be based on ability and effort — not social class. Sociologists question whether schools actually achieve this.

questions.question: Cultural capital gives middle-class pupils an educational advantage.
questions.explanation: Middle-class pupils' knowledge of cultural norms, language, and attitudes matches what schools value — giving them a built-in advantage.

questions.question: The hidden curriculum refers to official exam syllabuses.
questions.explanation: The hidden curriculum is the unofficial lessons schools teach — conformity, punctuality, deference to authority — serving middle-class values.

questions.question: Labelling can lead to a self-fulfilling prophecy.
questions.explanation: A negative label can cause a pupil to internalise it and behave accordingly — confirming the original label.

questions.question: Material deprivation only affects achievement through lack of textbooks.
questions.explanation: Material deprivation affects achievement through poor diet, overcrowded housing, inability to afford equipment, and stress from family financial problems.

questions.question: Girls consistently underperform compared to boys in GCSE results.
questions.explanation: Girls consistently OUTPERFORM boys at GCSE and A-level. The gender gap in education now favours girls.

questions.question: Institutional racism refers to individual teachers' prejudiced attitudes.
questions.explanation: Institutional racism refers to organisational policies and practices that systematically disadvantage minority groups — regardless of individual intent.

questions.question: Willis found working-class boys enthusiastically embraced school values.
questions.explanation: Willis found the opposite — working-class 'lads' rejected school values and formed anti-school subcultures, ironically confirming their class position.

questions.question: Streaming can reinforce existing inequalities.
questions.explanation: Pupils in lower streams receive lower expectations and less challenging work — the stream can become a self-fulfilling prophecy.

questions.question: Bourdieu coined the term 'cultural capital'.
questions.explanation: Bourdieu used cultural capital to explain why middle-class children succeed in education — their habitus matches the school's values.

questions.question: ________ is the idea that success in education is based on ability and effort — not background.
questions.explanation: Sociologists question whether education truly achieves meritocracy — class, gender, and ethnicity all affect outcomes.

questions.question: ________ capital is Bourdieu's concept — middle-class knowledge and attitudes valued by schools.
questions.explanation: Cultural capital includes familiarity with language, art, and cultural references that schools reward.

questions.question: ________ deprivation is the lack of money and resources affecting working-class pupils' achievement.
questions.explanation: Poor housing, diet, and inability to afford resources all disadvantage working-class pupils.

questions.question: A ________ prophecy occurs when a label causes behaviour that confirms the label.
questions.explanation: If labelled as disruptive, a pupil may internalise this and act accordingly — confirming the teacher's original judgement.

questions.question: The ________ curriculum teaches conformity, punctuality, and acceptance of authority.
questions.explanation: The hidden curriculum reinforces middle-class values and prepares pupils to accept hierarchy.

questions.question: ________ theory shows anti-school subcultures can confirm working-class pupils' class position.
questions.explanation: Willis's 'lads' rejected school but ended up in working-class jobs — reproducing their own position.

questions.question: ________ racism is when institutional policies systematically disadvantage ethnic minorities.
questions.explanation: Institutional racism operates through structures and norms — not necessarily through individual prejudice.

questions.question: Girls outperform boys partly due to a 'laddish' ________ culture among boys.
questions.explanation: 'Laddish' culture makes academic effort seem unmasculine — contributing to boys' underachievement.

questions.question: ________ groups pupils by ability across all subjects — can reinforce inequalities.
questions.explanation: Streaming can become self-fulfilling — lower-stream pupils receive lower expectations and less support.

questions.question: ________ deprivation combined with cultural capital differences explain class attainment gaps.
questions.explanation: Working-class pupils face both a lack of resources and a mismatch between their cultural background and school expectations.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: success based on ability and effort — not background
middle-class knowledge giving educational advantage
fixed definition attached to pupil — can become self-fulfilling
informal lessons in conformity serving middle-class values
label causes behaviour that confirms it

cards.definition: an act that breaks the law of the land — subject to formal punishment
behaviour that breaks social norms — does not have to be criminal (e.g. bad manners)
Durkheim — crime is inevitable and serves functions: boundary maintenance, social solidarity, and enabling change
Merton — crime results from a gap between culturally valued goals (wealth) and the legitimate means to achieve them
Cohen — working-class boys who fail educationally form delinquent subcultures as an alternative status system
crime is defined by the powerful to protect their interests — white-collar crime is under-prosecuted
Becker — defining someone as criminal leads to a deviant career — the label becomes a master status
mechanisms that keep people conforming to social norms — formal (police, courts) or informal (peer pressure)
crime committed by high-status individuals in the course of their work — e.g. fraud, embezzlement
Cohen — media exaggeration of a social problem creates disproportionate public fear and a folk devil

questions.question: What is the difference between crime and deviance?
options: A) They are the same thing
B) Crime breaks social norms; deviance breaks the law
C) Deviance breaks social norms but may not be illegal; crime breaks the law
D) Deviance is always more serious than crime
questions.explanation: Crime is illegal behaviour. Deviance breaks social norms but may be legal — e.g. extreme body modification or nudism in certain contexts.

questions.question: What does Durkheim say about crime?
options: A) Crime must be eliminated
B) Crime is inevitable and serves positive functions for society
C) Crime is caused by inequality
D) Crime is a product of labelling
questions.explanation: Durkheim argued crime is universal — every society has it. It serves functions: boundary maintenance (clarifying norms), social solidarity (uniting against deviance), and enabling social change.

questions.question: What is Merton's strain theory?
options: A) Crime results from lack of intelligence
B) Crime results from a gap between culturally valued goals and the legitimate means to achieve them
C) Crime is caused by weak social bonds
D) Crime is learnt from criminal peers
questions.explanation: Merton argued American culture promotes wealth as a goal for all, but legitimate means (education, jobs) are unequally distributed — some people turn to crime when they cannot succeed legitimately.

questions.question: What is a moral panic?
options: A) A genuine public health emergency
B) Media exaggeration creating disproportionate fear about a social problem and a 'folk devil'
C) A government crime prevention campaign
D) Public concern about rising crime statistics
questions.explanation: Cohen's moral panic: media sensationalises a threat → police crackdown → creates more deviants → confirms original fear. Mods and Rockers were his original 'folk devils'.

questions.question: What does labelling theory say about crime?
options: A) Criminals are born, not made
B) Defining someone as criminal creates a deviant career — the label becomes their identity
C) Crime is caused by poverty
D) Criminals choose crime rationally
questions.explanation: Becker argued the label 'criminal' becomes a master status — affecting how others see you and how you see yourself — leading to a deviant career.

questions.question: What is white-collar crime?
options: A) Crime committed by teenagers
B) Violent crime in city centres
C) Crime by high-status individuals in the course of their work
D) Property crime in wealthy areas
questions.explanation: White-collar crime (Sutherland) includes fraud, embezzlement, and corporate crime. Marxists argue it is under-prosecuted because the powerful define and enforce the law.

questions.question: What does Marxist criminology argue?
options: A) Crime is caused by socialisation failures
B) Crime serves positive social functions
C) The law is defined by the powerful to protect their interests
D) Crime is inevitable in all societies
questions.explanation: Marxists argue the law protects ruling-class interests — working-class crimes (theft) are heavily prosecuted while white-collar and corporate crimes are largely ignored.

questions.question: What is Cohen's subcultural theory?
options: A) Middle-class youths create delinquent subcultures
B) Working-class boys who fail form delinquent subcultures as an alternative status system
C) Crime is caused by weak family bonds
D) Subcultures always lead to serious crime
questions.explanation: Cohen argued working-class boys who fail in school cannot achieve status legitimately — so they create alternative subcultures where delinquency provides status.

questions.question: What is social control?
options: A) The government's control of the economy
B) Mechanisms keeping people conforming — formal (law) and informal (peer pressure)
C) Control of the media by the state
D) Control of deviant behaviour through punishment only
questions.explanation: Social control includes formal mechanisms (police, courts, prisons) and informal ones (family disapproval, peer pressure, religious teachings) that keep behaviour within norms.

questions.question: What is a 'master status' in labelling theory?
options: A) A high-status individual in society
B) A label that overrides all other aspects of identity
C) A teacher's assessment of a pupil
D) A status achieved through hard work
questions.explanation: A master status (Becker) dominates all other characteristics — once labelled 'criminal', that label defines how others see you, overriding all other aspects of your identity.

questions.question: All deviance is criminal.
questions.explanation: Deviance breaks social norms but is not necessarily illegal. Crime is specifically the breaking of laws. E.g. bad manners are deviant but not criminal.

questions.question: Durkheim argued crime is dysfunctional and should be eliminated.
questions.explanation: Durkheim argued crime is INEVITABLE and FUNCTIONAL — it clarifies norms, promotes solidarity, and can enable social change.

questions.question: Merton's strain theory argues crime results from poverty alone.
questions.explanation: Merton argued crime results from a gap between culturally valued GOALS (wealth) and the MEANS to achieve them — not poverty alone.

questions.question: White-collar crime receives more police attention than street crime.
questions.explanation: White-collar crime is typically under-prosecuted — Marxists argue this is because the powerful define and enforce the law in their own interests.

questions.question: Labelling can lead to a deviant career.
questions.explanation: Once labelled criminal, individuals may internalise the label — it becomes a master status affecting their self-concept and interactions.

questions.question: A moral panic involves proportionate media coverage of a real threat.
questions.explanation: A moral panic involves DISPROPORTIONATE media coverage — exaggerating a threat, creating a 'folk devil', and amplifying the problem.

questions.question: Cohen's subcultural theory focuses on working-class boys who fail in education.
questions.explanation: Cohen argued working-class boys who cannot achieve status through education form delinquent subcultures as an alternative status system.

questions.question: Social control includes both formal and informal mechanisms.
questions.explanation: Formal control: police, courts, prisons. Informal control: family, peers, media, religion.

questions.question: Marxists argue the law protects the interests of the ruling class.
questions.explanation: Laws against property crime protect ruling-class wealth; corporate crime (harming workers) is often less severely prosecuted.

questions.question: A 'folk devil' is created when media sensationalises a social group as a threat.
questions.explanation: Cohen showed how Mods and Rockers were turned into 'folk devils' by sensationalist media coverage.

questions.question: ________ breaks social norms but may not be illegal; crime specifically breaks the law.
questions.explanation: Deviance and crime overlap but are not identical — something can be deviant without being criminal.

questions.question: Durkheim argued crime is ________ — it serves boundary maintenance and social solidarity.
questions.explanation: Crime clarifies norms when it is punished — and can enable social change by challenging existing laws.

questions.question: Merton's ________ theory argues crime results from a gap between goals and means.
questions.explanation: When legitimate means to success are blocked, some people adapt through crime.

questions.question: ________ theory argues defining someone as criminal creates a deviant career.
questions.explanation: The label becomes a master status — shaping self-concept and others' responses.

questions.question: A ________ panic is media exaggeration of a threat — creating a 'folk devil'.
questions.explanation: Stanley Cohen developed moral panic theory through his study of Mods and Rockers.

questions.question: ________ crime is committed by high-status individuals in the course of their work.
questions.explanation: Fraud, embezzlement, and corporate crime are examples of white-collar crime.

questions.question: ________ argue the law is defined by the powerful to protect their interests.
questions.explanation: Corporate crime harms many people but is under-prosecuted compared to working-class crime.

questions.question: Cohen's ________ theory: working-class boys form delinquent subcultures after educational failure.
questions.explanation: Status frustration drives working-class boys to form alternative peer groups that reward delinquency.

questions.question: ________ control includes both the police and courts (formal) and family and peers (informal).
questions.explanation: Social control mechanisms keep most people conforming to norms most of the time.

questions.question: A ________ status is a label that overrides all other aspects of identity.
questions.explanation: Once labelled 'criminal', this label dominates how others perceive the individual.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: breaks the law — subject to formal punishment
breaks social norms — may not be criminal
crime is inevitable and functional for society
media exaggeration creates folk devil and disproportionate fear
criminal label becomes master status — creates deviant career

cards.definition: a set of beliefs and practices relating to the sacred or supernatural — performs social functions
Durkheim — religion promotes social solidarity and collective identity; the sacred is society worshipping itself
religion is the 'opium of the masses' — it legitimises inequality and prevents revolution
religion is patriarchal — it oppresses women through sacred texts, leadership, and norms
the claim that religion is declining in modern societies — challenged by the persistence of faith globally
spiritual beliefs and practices outside mainstream religion — individualised, consumer-driven spirituality
a return to strict, literal interpretation of religious texts — often a reaction to modernity and globalisation
the existence of many different religions in a society — associated with modernity and tolerance
minority ethnic groups often show higher levels of religious participation — religion as community and cultural identity
religion can promote social change — Martin Luther King used Christianity to advance civil rights

questions.question: What does Durkheim argue about religion?
options: A) Religion is the opium of the masses
B) Religion is patriarchal
C) Religion promotes social solidarity — the sacred is society worshipping itself
D) Religion is declining in modern societies
questions.explanation: Durkheim studied Australian Aboriginal totemism — arguing that religion binds communities together and the sacred represents the collective power of society.

questions.question: What does Marx say about religion?
options: A) Religion helps the working class
B) Religion is the opium of the masses — it legitimises inequality and prevents revolution
C) Religion promotes social change
D) Religion is declining in modern societies
questions.explanation: Marx: religion comforts the oppressed ('opium') and tells them their suffering is God's will or will be rewarded in heaven — preventing them from rebelling against capitalism.

questions.question: What is the secularisation thesis?
options: A) Religion is growing globally
B) Religion is becoming more diverse
C) Religion is declining in influence in modern societies
D) Religion has always been in decline
questions.explanation: The secularisation thesis argues modernisation, science, and individualisation have undermined religion's influence. Evidence: falling church attendance, fewer religious marriages.

questions.question: What is fundamentalism?
options: A) A return to moderate religious practice
B) Rejecting all religion
C) Strict, literal interpretation of religious texts — often a reaction to modernity
D) New Age spirituality
questions.explanation: Religious fundamentalism (Giddens) is a response to the uncertainty of modernity — returning to absolute truths and strict rules.

questions.question: What does feminist sociology say about religion?
options: A) Religion benefits women
B) Religion is declining
C) Religion is patriarchal — it oppresses women through texts, leadership, and norms
D) Religion has no gender dimension
questions.explanation: Feminist sociologists argue most world religions are patriarchal: sacred texts are male-authored, leadership is male-dominated, and norms restrict women's freedom.

questions.question: What is the significance of ethnicity and religion?
options: A) Ethnic minorities have lower religious participation
B) Minority ethnic groups often have higher religious participation — religion provides community and identity
C) Religion has no relationship with ethnicity
D) Only White British people are religious
questions.explanation: For minority ethnic groups, religion can be a source of cultural identity and community — especially in societies where they face discrimination.

questions.question: Can religion promote social change?
options: A) No — religion always supports the status quo
B) Only in non-Western societies
C) Yes — e.g. the Civil Rights Movement used Christianity to argue for equality
D) Only through fundamentalist movements
questions.explanation: Religion can inspire change — Martin Luther King used Christian moral arguments to advance civil rights. Liberation theology in Latin America also used religion to challenge oppression.

questions.question: What are New Age movements?
options: A) A type of fundamentalism
B) Mainstream religious denominations
C) Individualised, consumer-driven spiritual beliefs outside mainstream religion
D) Government religious policies
questions.explanation: New Age movements (yoga, crystals, astrology) reflect individualised spirituality — people pick and choose beliefs rather than following one religion.

questions.question: What is religious pluralism?
options: A) The decline of all religion
B) One dominant religion in a society
C) The existence of many different religions in a society
D) Religious fundamentalism
questions.explanation: Religious pluralism describes multi-faith societies — associated with tolerance and modernity, though some argue pluralism undermines religion's authority.

questions.question: What is Stark and Bainbridge's religious market theory?
options: A) Religion is declining
B) Religion is patriarchal
C) Religious participation is driven by demand — people choose religions like goods in a market
D) Religion promotes inequality
questions.explanation: Stark and Bainbridge argue that where religious diversity exists (a free 'market'), people have more choice and participation is higher — challenging the secularisation thesis.

questions.question: Durkheim argued religion promotes social solidarity and collective identity.
questions.explanation: Durkheim studied totemism and argued religion binds communities — the sacred represents the collective power of society.

questions.question: Marx saw religion as a force for positive social change.
questions.explanation: Marx saw religion as the 'opium of the masses' — it dulled the pain of exploitation and prevented revolution.

questions.question: The secularisation thesis argues religion is growing in all modern societies.
questions.explanation: The secularisation thesis argues religion is DECLINING in influence — though this is contested, especially given global religious growth.

questions.question: Feminists argue all religions are equally oppressive to women.
questions.explanation: Feminists argue most mainstream religions are patriarchal, but the degree varies. Some argue women can find empowerment within religion.

questions.question: Minority ethnic groups generally show higher levels of religious participation.
questions.explanation: Research consistently shows higher religious participation among minority ethnic groups — religion serves as community identity and cultural preservation.

questions.question: Fundamentalism is a relaxed, modern approach to religion.
questions.explanation: Fundamentalism is the strict, literal interpretation of religious texts — often a defensive reaction to perceived threats from modernity.

questions.question: Religion can only act as a conservative force — never promoting change.
questions.explanation: Religion has both conservative and radical potential. The Civil Rights Movement and liberation theology both used religion to challenge injustice.

questions.question: New Age movements represent highly organised, communal religious practice.
questions.explanation: New Age movements are highly individualised — people pick and choose spiritual beliefs rather than joining organised religions.

questions.question: Religious pluralism describes a society with only one dominant religion.
questions.explanation: Religious pluralism describes a society with many different religions coexisting.

questions.question: Weber argued Protestantism contributed to the development of capitalism.
questions.explanation: Weber's 'Protestant Ethic' argued Calvinist values (hard work, thrift, calling) created conditions favourable to capitalist development.

questions.question: Durkheim argued religion promotes social ________ — the sacred is society worshipping itself.
questions.explanation: Religion binds communities together through shared beliefs, rituals, and a sense of the sacred.

questions.question: Marx called religion the 'opium of the ________' — it prevents revolution by legitimising inequality.
questions.explanation: Religion comforts the oppressed and tells them suffering is God's will — discouraging resistance.

questions.question: The ________ thesis argues religion is declining in influence in modern societies.
questions.explanation: Evidence includes falling church attendance, fewer religious marriages, and declining religious belief.

questions.question: ________ is the strict, literal interpretation of religious texts — a reaction to modernity.
questions.explanation: Fundamentalist movements often reject modern values and scientific explanation.

questions.question: Feminist sociologists argue most religions are ________ — they oppress women.
questions.explanation: Sacred texts, male leadership, and restrictive norms all reflect and reinforce patriarchy.

questions.question: For minority ethnic groups, religion often provides ________ identity and community.
questions.explanation: Religion helps maintain cultural heritage and provides community support in societies where minorities may face discrimination.

questions.question: Martin Luther ________ used Christian moral arguments to advance civil rights — showing religion can promote change.
questions.explanation: This challenges the view that religion is always a conservative force.

questions.question: ________ Age movements offer individualised, consumer-driven spirituality outside mainstream religion.
questions.explanation: People mix beliefs from different traditions rather than following one organised religion.

questions.question: Religious ________ describes a multi-faith society with many different religions coexisting.
questions.explanation: Pluralism is associated with tolerance and modernity — though some argue it undermines religious authority.

questions.question: Weber argued ________ work ethic contributed to the development of capitalism.
questions.explanation: Calvinist values of hard work, frugality, and a 'calling' created conditions for capitalist accumulation.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: promotes social solidarity — sacred is society worshipping itself
opium of the masses — legitimises inequality
religion is patriarchal — oppresses women
religion declining in modern societies
strict literal interpretation — reaction to modernity
